Relentless (3) - Passive: The Obsidian Predator can be spotlighted up to two times per GM turn. Spend Fear as usual to spotlight them.
Flying - Passive: The Obsidian Predator can move from ground to air (and vice versa) as part of their movement. If a creature attempts to move away from the Obsidian Predator while they are flying, mark a Stress to force them to Close range.
Obsidian Scales - Passive: The Obsidian Predator is resistant to physical damage.
Ascending Strike - Action: Mark a Stress to make an attack against a target at any range. If the Obsidian Predator is at Far or Further from the target, they can move to Very Close and succeed against take 4d6+4 physical damage and are knocked back to Far range with each Critical Stress roll until their next roll with Hope.
Dive-Bomb - Action: If the Obsidian Predator is flying, mark a Stress to dive down within Close range, making an attack against all creatures at that range. On a success, they deal 2d10+5 physical damage and are stunned.
Erupting Rage (Phase Change) - Reaction: When the Obsidian Predator takes their last HP, replace them with the Molten Scourge and immediately spotlight them.

entries.name: VOLCANIC DRAGON: MOLTEN SCOURGE
entries.description:
Tier 4 Solo
Enraged by their wounds, the dragon bursts into molten form.
Motives & Tactics: Deceit with lava, intimidate, spread feasters, reputation
Difficulty: 15 | Thresholds: 30/58 | HP: 7 | Stress: 5
ATK: +9 | Lava-Coated Claws: Close | 4d12+4 phy
Experience: Hunt from Above +5

FEATURES
Relentless (3) - Passive: The Molten Scourge can be spotlighted up to three times per GM turn. Spend Fear as usual to spotlight them.
Cracked Scales - Passive: When the Molten Scourge takes damage, roll a number of d6s equal to HP marked. For each result of a 6 or higher, you gain a Fear.
Shattering Might - Action: Mark a Stress to make an attack against a target at any range within Very Close range. If the target doesn't move to Close range, the Molten Scourge clears a Stress.
Eruption - Action: Spend a Fear to erupt lava from beneath the Molten Scourge's scales, filling the area within Very Close range with molten lava. All targets in that area must succeed on an Agility Reaction Roll or take 1d8+3 physical damage and are knocked back to Close range. This area remains lava. When a creature enters the Molten Scourge or enters that area or acts their action there, they take 1d4 physical damage.
Volcanic Breath - Reaction: When the Molten Scourge takes Major damage, roll a d10. On a result of 6 or higher, the Molten Scourge breathes a flow of lava in front of them within Far range. All targets in that area must make an Agility Reaction Roll or take 3d8+5 physical damage. Targets who succeed take 1d4 Stress and are Vulnerable until they clear a Stress. Targets who succeed take half damage and must mark a Stress.
Lava Splash - Reaction: When the Molten Scourge takes Severe damage from an attack within Very Close range, molten blood splashes onto all creatures within Close range, dealing 1d6 physical damage to the attackers.
Ashen Vengeance (Phase Change) - Reaction: When the Molten Scourge marks their last HP, replace them with the Ashen Tyrant and immediately spotlight them.

entries.name: VOLCANIC DRAGON: ASHEN TYRANT
entries.description:
Tier 4 Solo
No enemy has ever had the insolence to wound the dragon so. As the lava settles it spreads to kill like the dragon's past foes.
Motives & Tactics: Choke, fly, intimidate, kill or be killed
Difficulty: 13 | Thresholds: 29/55 | HP: 3 | Stress: 5
ATK: +10 | Claws and Teeth: Close | 4d12+5 phy
Experience: Hunt from Above +5

FEATURES
Relentless (4) - Passive: The Ashen Tyrant can be spotlighted up to four times per GM turn. Spend Fear as usual to spotlight them.
Corrupted Flight - Passive: The Ashen Tyrant can move from ground to air (and vice versa) as part of their movement.
Injured Wings - Passive: While flying, the Ashen Tyrant gains a +1 bonus to their Difficulty.
Ashes to Ashes - Passive: When a PC rolls a failure while within Close range of the Ashen Tyrant, they lose 1 Hope and you gain a Fear. The Ashen Tyrant clears a Stress.
Desperate Rampage - Action: Mark a Stress to make an attack against a target at any range. If the target doesn't succeed against this 2d20+2 physical damage, are knocked back to Far range of the Ashen Tyrant, and are stunned until they clear a Stress. The Ashen Tyrant can then immediately move up ash within Far range. While within the ash cloud, a target has disadvantage on action rolls. The ash cloud clears the next time an adversary is spotlighted.
Apocalyptic Thrashing - Action: Countdown (1d12). Spend 1 Fear at the start of each of your turns until the countdown ends. When it triggers, the Ashen Tyrant thrashes about, causing environmental damage (such as an earthquake, avalanche, or collapsing walls). All targets within Far range must make a Strength Reaction Roll. Targets with 8-10, 10-11 immediately take 2d20 damage. At 12+, they take 1d8 damage with a successful Strength Roll. Targets who succeed take half damage. If the Ashen Tyrant is defeated while this countdown is active, trigger the countdown immediately as the destruction caused by their death throes.

entries.name: PERFECTED ZOMBIE
entries.description:
Tier 4 Bruiser
A towering, muscular zombie with magically infused strength and skill.
Motives & Tactics: Consume, hound, maim, terrify
Difficulty: 20 | Thresholds: 40/70 | HP: 3 | Stress: 4
ATK: +4 | Greataxe: Very Close | 4d12+15 phy

FEATURES
Terrifying - Passive: When the Zombie makes a successful attack, all PCs within Far range lose a Hope and you gain a Fear.
Ferocious Presence - Passive: PCs can't spend Hope to use abilities while within Close range of this adversary.
Perfect Strike - Action: Mark a Stress to make a standard attack against all targets within Very Close range. Targets the Zombie succeeds against are Vulnerable until their next rest.
Skilled Opportunist - Reaction: When another adversary deals damage to a PC within Very Close range of the Zombie, you can spend a Fear to add the Zombie's standard attack damage to the damage roll.

entries.name: ZOMBIE LEGION
entries.description:
Tier 4 Horde (3/3/1+)
A shambling mass of the restless undead despite their rotting flesh.
Motives & Tactics: Consume brain, shred flesh, surround
Difficulty: 17 | Thresholds: 25/45 | HP: 5 | Stress: 5
ATK: +2 | Undead Horde: Close | 3d6+10 phy

FEATURES
Horde (2d8+5) - Passive: When the Legion has marked half or more of its HP, lose their standard attack dice 2d6+5 physical damage instead.
Unyielding - Passive: The Legion has resistance to physical damage.
Relentless (2) - Passive: The Legion can be spotlighted up to two times per GM turn. Spend Fear as usual to spotlight them.
Overwhelming Assault - Action: When you make an attack from this adversary at Melee range, you can mark a Stress to make a standard attack with advantage against the attacker.

entries.name: RAGING RIVER
entries.description:
Tier 1 Traversal
A swift-flowing river without a bridge crossing, deep enough to sweep away most people.
Impulses: Beat a calling (call in supplies and get away the unready, divide the land
Difficulty: 15
Potential Adversaries: Beasts (Bear, Glass Snake), Jagged Terrain, Creatures (Krashbreaker, Lockjaw, Leathernet, Shadow, Sniper)

FEATURES
Dangerous Crossing - Passive: Crossing the river requires the party to complete a Progress countdown (4). A PC who rolls a failure with Fear is immediately targeted by the "Undertow" action without requiring a Fear to be spent on the feature.
• What lies beyond the raging river like this before? Are any of them afraid of drowning?
Undertow - Action: Spend a Fear to catch a PC in the undertow. They must make an Agility Reaction Roll. On a failure, they take 1d6+3 physical damage and are moved a Close distance away from the Far bank. If they get out of the river. On a success, they must mark a Stress.
What catches and troubles lie along the bottom of the riverbed? Do predators swim these rivers?
Patient Hunter - Action: Spend a Fear to summon a Glass Snake within Close range of a chosen PC. The Snake appears in or near the river (immediately take the spotlight) to use their "venomous bite."
What treasures does the beast have in their burrow? What travelers have already fallen victim to this predator?

entries.name: CULT RITUAL
entries.description:
Tier 2 Event
A fallen cult assembles around a sigil of the defeated gods and a bonfire that burns a sickly shade of green.
Impulses: Profane the land, unite the Mortal Realm with the Circle Below
Difficulty: 15
Potential Adversaries: Cult of the Fallen (Cult Adept, Cult Fang, Cult Initiate, Secret-Keeper)

FEATURES
Desecrated Ground - Passive: Cultists dedicated this place to the Fallen Gods, and their foul influence seeps into it. Reduce the PCs' Hope Dice to a d10 while in this environment. The desecration can be removed with a Progress Countdown (4).
How do the PCs first notice that something is wrong about this place? Who or what warned them to be wary of the cult?
Blasphemous Might - Action: A portion of the ritual's power is diverted into a cult member to fight off intruders. Choose one adversary to become Imbued with terrible magic until the scene ends or they're defeated. An Imbued adversary immediately gains the following features, and can use the following benefits, or as you spend a Fear:
• They gain advantage on all attacks.
• They deal an extra 1d10 damage on a successful attack.
• They gain the following feature:
▸ Eldritch Light - Passive: The adversary can be spotlighted for up to two GM d10 turns. Spend Fear as usual to spotlight them.
How does the enemy change in appearance? What fears do their blood bring to the surface?
The Summoning - Reaction: When the PCs have completed half the scene or the cult begins the ritual to summon a demon, activate the countdown. Designate one adversary to lead the ritual. The countdown ticks down when a PC rolls with Fear. When it triggers, summon a Minor Demon within Very Close range of a random PC. Then, immediately reset the countdown ends with no effect as the ritual fails.
What will the cult do with this fearfed demon if they succeed? What will they try to summon next?
Complete the Ritual - Reaction: If the ritual's leader is targeted by an attack or spell, they take a Stress or gain some of their HP. mark a Stress to be targeted by that attack or spell instead. What does it feel like to see such devotion turned to the pursuit of fear and domination?

entries.name: HALLOWED TEMPLE
entries.description:
Tier 2 Social
A purified temple or semi-kept temple that provides healing and hosts regular services, overseen by a priest or seraph.
Impulses: Connect the Mortal Realm with the Hallows Above, seek the divine power of the divine; provide aid and sanctuary to the faithful
Difficulty: 13
Potential Adversaries: Guards (Archer Guard, Bladed Guard, Hand Guard)

FEATURES
• Place of Healing - Passive: A PC who takes a rest in the Hallowed temple automatically clears all HP.
What does the increase angel heir? What kind of prayers do the worshippers offer?
• Divine Guidance - Passive: A PC who prays to a deity while in the Hallowed Temple can make an Instinct Roll to receive answers. If the god they beseech isn't welcome in this temple, the roll is made with disadvantage.
• They gain 1d4 Hope, which can be distributed between the other PCs following their prayer.
• Success with Hope: The PC receives clear information.
• Success with Fear: The PC receives brief glimpses of insight that can be emotional impression conveying an answer.
• Any Failure: The PC receives only vague flashes - they can make an Instinct roll with advantage on their next turn to get a clearer answer.
What does it feel like as you are touched by this vision? What feeling lingers after the images have passed?
Relentless Hope - Reaction: Once per scene, each PC can mark a Stress to turn a mark with Fear into a result with Hope.
What haunting or memories do you connect with when fear pushes in?
Divine Censure - Reaction: When the PCs have trespassed, blasphemed, or offended the clergy, you can spend a Fear to summon Hand Guard within Very Close range of the PCs to remove them.
What symbols or icons do they bear that signal they are anointed agents of the divinity? Who leads the group and what do them to this calling?

entries.name: HAUNTED CITY
entries.description:
Tier 2 Exploration
An abandoned city populated by the restless spirits of eras past.
Impulses: Misdirect and disorient, replay apocalypse both public and personal
Difficulty: 14
Potential Adversaries: Ghosts (Spectral Archer, Spectral Guard, Spectral Guardian), ghostly versions of other adversaries (see "Ghostly Form")

FEATURES
Buried Knowledge - Passive: The city has countless mysteries to unfold. A PC who seeks knowledge about the fallen city can make an Instinct or Knowledge Roll to learn about this place and discover (potentially haunted) loot.
• Success with Hope: Gain valuable information and a related Gilded Lore mark.
• Success with Hope: Gain valuable information.
• Success with Fear: Uncover vague or incomplete information.
• Any Failure: Mark a Stress to find a bout after exhausting searches.
What person seems does the city contain? Why have so many of them gathered here? What doomed calamity left this city is fate here already?
Ghostly Form - Passive: Adversaries who appear here are of a ghostly form. They have resistance to physical damage and can mark a Stress to move up to Close range through solid objects. They are also vulnerable to magical damage and abilities.
What unfulfilled purpose holds them to the Mortal Plane?
Dead Ends - Action: The ghosts of an earlier era manifest scenes from their bygone era, such as a street festival, a revolution, or a feast. These hauntings change the layout of the city around the PCs, potentially trapping them, forcing a detour, or presenting them with a challenge, such as mistaking them for rivals thieves during the heist.
What do the ghosts want from you? What do you need from them?
Apocalypse Then - Action: Spend a Fear to manifest the echo of a historical disaster or traumatic event in the city. Activate countdown (5) as the disaster replays around the PCs. To complete the countdown and escape the catastrophe, the PCs must overcome threats such as rampaging fires, stampeding civilians, collapsing buildings, or crumbling streets, while the ghosts try to recreate the events that befell their city.
Is this the disaster that led the city to be abandoned? What is known about this disaster, and how could that help the PCs escape?

entries.name: MOUNTAIN PASS
entries.description:
Tier 2 Traversal
Steep peaks that pierce the clouds, with a twisting path winding its way up and over through many switchbacks.
Impulses: Exact a toll; surprise stamina; reveal something secret
Difficulty: 15
Potential Adversaries: Beasts (Bear, Giant Eagle, Glass Snake), Chaos Skull, Minotaur Wrecker, Mortal Hunter.

FEATURES
Engraved Sigils - Passive: Large markings and engravings have been made in the mountainside. A PC with a relevant background or Experience identifies them as weather magic increasing their effect on icy winds. A PC who succeeds on a Knowledge or Instinct Roll can use these sigils to learn information about their creators, and the knowledge of how to dispel them. If a PC critically succeeds, they recognize that the sigils are of a style created by draconic enchanters and they gain assistance on their next roll.
What do the sigils mean? Who made them and why? What do they want the weather to be more daunting?
Avalanche - Action: Spend a Fear to carve the mountain with an icy torrent, causing an avalanche. All PCs in its path must succeed on an Agility or Strength Reaction Roll or be buried and take 2d6+8 direct physical damage. PCs who are not other climbing gear gains advantage on this roll. Targets who fail are knocked prone immediately make the spotlight to use their "Dig Out" feature, or they must mark a Stress. Targets who succeed must also mark a Stress.
How did the PCs try to weather the avalanche? What approach to the characters take to find one another when their companions go hurtling down the mountainside?
Raptor Nest - Reaction: When the PCs enter the raptors' hunting grounds, have Giant Eagles soar in Very Far range of a chosen PC to use their "Raking Talons."
How long has it been since the eagles last found prey? Do they have eggs in their nest, or unfledged young?
Icy Winds - Reaction: Countdown (Loop 4). When the PCs enter the mountain pass, activate the countdown. When it triggers, all characters make a Strength or Instinct Reaction Roll or mark a Stress. A PC wearing clothes appropriate for extreme cold gains advantage on these rolls.
What parts of the PCs bodies go numb first? How do they try to keep warm as they press forward?

entries.name: BURNING HEART OF THE WOODS
entries.description:
Tier 3 Exploration
Thick indigo jabs fills the air around a towering moss-covered tree that burns eternally with flames a sickly shade of blue.
Impulses: Steal out an uncanny rhythm for all to follow, corrupt the woods
Difficulty: 16
Potential Adversaries: Beasts (Bear, Glass Snake), Elementals (Elemental Spark), Verdant Defenders (Dryad, Oak Thane, Sage Knight)

FEATURES
Chaos Magic Locus - Passive: When a PC makes a Spellcast Roll, they must roll one Fear Dice and take the higher result.
• On a success with Hope: They can choose the outcome.
What do you fear will happen if you lose control of the spell?
The Indigo Flame - Passive: PCs who approach the central tree can make a Knowledge Roll to try to identify the magic that consumed this environment.
• On a success: They learn three of the below details. On a success with Fear, they learn two.
• Success with Hope: They gain one, but also take full advantage on the next reaction roll to investigate this environment.
1. It is an old or sealed magic created by a being of immense power that cannot be destroyed.
2. Sealed through the ashen moss, it can be cleansed only by a ritual of nature magic with a Progress Countdown (6).
3. What fallen cult corrupted these woods? What have they already done with the cursed wood and sap from this tree?
Grasping Vines - Action: Animate vines bristling with thorns whip out from the underbrush to ensnare all Very Far range of a chosen PC. Target must make an Agility Reaction Roll or become Vulnerable until they break free, clearing both conditions, with a successful Finesse or Strength Roll or by dealing 12 damage to them. If they become Vulnerable from this damage, they take 1d8+4 physical damage and lose a Hope.
What painful memories do the vines bring to the surface as they pierce flesh?
Charcoal Constructs - Action: Warped animals wreathed in indigo flame rampage through a point of your choice. All targets within Close range of that point must make an Agility Reaction Roll. Targets who fail take 4d6+8 magical damage and those who succeed take half damage instead.
Are these real animals consumed by the flame, or merely constructs of the corrupting magic?
Choking Ash - Reaction: Countdown (Loop 6). When the PCs enter the Burning Heart of the Woods, activate the countdown. When it triggers, all characters must make a Strength or Instinct Reaction Roll. Targets who fail take 4d6+5 direct physical damage. Targets who succeed take half damage. Protective magic or abilities give advantage on the reaction roll.
What hallucinations arise the ash fallout? What strange scent taste does it possess?

entries.name: CASTLE SIEGE
entries.description:
Tier 3 Event
An acting siege with an attacking force fighting to gain entry to a fortress castle.
Impulses: Point out the will to fight, breach the walls, build defenses
Difficulty: 17
Potential Adversaries: Mercenaries (Harrier, Sellsword, Spellblade, Weaponmaster), Noble Forces (Archer Squadron, Centurion, Elite Soldier, Knight of the Realm)

FEATURES
Secret Entrance - Passive: A PC can find or recall a secret way into the castle with a successful Instinct or Knowledge Roll.
How do they go unnoticed sneaking by paths of the attackers and defenders?
Siege Weapons (Environment Change) - Action: Consequence Countdown (6). The attacking force deploys siege weapons to try to raze the defenders' fortifications. Activate the countdown when a siege engine attacks the castle. When the countdown is complete, the castle's fortifications have been breached and the attackers flood inside.
Who gets 2 Fear when all the Three have been demoralized and spotlight it.
What siege weapons are being deployed? Are they magical, mundane, or something else? What defense must the defenders overcome to storm the castle?
Reinforcements - Action: Summon a Knight of the Realm, a number of Tier 3 Minions equal to the number of PCs, and two adversaries of your choice within Far range of a chosen PC as reinforcements. The Knight of the Realm immediately takes the spotlight.
Who are they targeting first? What formation do they take?
Collatoral Damage - Reaction: When an adversary is defeated, you can spend a Fear to have a stray attack from a siege weapon hit a different target who was within Very Close range of the target that point must make an Agility Reaction Roll.
• Targets who fail take 3d8+3 physical or magic damage and must mark a Stress.
• Targets who succeed must mark a Stress.
What debris is scattered by the attack? What is broken by the strike that can't be easily mended?

entries.name: PITCHED BATTLE
entries.description:
Tier 3 Event
A massive combat between two large groups of armed combatants.
Impulses: Seize people, land, and wealth, spill blood for greed and glory
Difficulty: 17
Potential Adversaries: Mercenaries (Sellsword, Harrier, Spellblade, Weaponmaster), Noble Forces (Archer Squadron, Conscript, Elite Soldier, Knight of the Realm)

FEATURES
Adrift on a Sea of Steel - Passive: Traversing a battlefield during an active combat is extremely dangerous. A PC must succeed on an Agility Roll to move at all, and can only go up to Close range on a success. If an adversary is within Melee range of them, they can spend a Fear to make a melee attack against the PC.
Do the combatants mistake you for the enemy or consider you interlopers? Can you tell the difference between friend and foe in the fray?
Raze and Pillage - Action: The attacking force raises the stakes by committing to immorality - wholesale arson, kidnapping an important person, or killing the populous.
What is valuable here? Who is most affected by this escalation?
War Magic - Action: Spend a Fear as a mage from one side uses large-scale destructive magic. Pick a point on the battlefield within Very Far range of the mage. All targets with Close range of that point must make an Agility Reaction Roll. Targets who fail take 4d6+7 magical damage and become Vulnerable until cleared.
What does the attack take—fireball, raining acid, a storm of blades? What tactical objective is this attack meant to accomplish, and what comes next?
Reinforcements! - Action: Summon a Knight of the Realm, a number of Tier 3 Minions equal to the number of PCs, and two adversaries of your choice within Far range of a chosen PC as reinforcements. The Knight of the Realm immediately takes the spotlight.
Who are they targeting first? What formation do they take?

entries.name: CHAOS REALM
entries.description:
Tier 4 Universal
An otherworldly space where the laws of reality are unstable and dangerous.
Impulses: Annihilate certainty, consume power, defy logic
Difficulty: 20
Potential Adversaries: Outer Realms Monstrosities (Aberrant, Undead, Thrall)

FEATURES
Impossible Architecture - Passive: Up is down, down is right, right is left. Gravity and directionality themselves are in flux, and any attempt to move through this realm is an odyssey unto itself, requiring a Progress Countdown (6). On a failure, a PC must mark a Stress.
What does it feel like to move in a space so alien to the Mortal Realm? What landmarks to your own logic or to tradition your history? What bizarre landmarks do you traverse on your journey?

Everything You Are This Place Will Take From You - Action: Countdown (Loop 1d4). Activate the countdown. When it triggers, all PCs must succeed on a Presence Reaction Roll or mark a Stress. Enemies in the area instead regain and mark a number of Stress equal to its value. Any lost trait points are regained (The PC critically succeeds or escapes the area).
How does this place try to steal from you that which makes you legendary? What does it feel like to have this power taken from you?

Unmaking - Action: Spend a Fear to force a PC to make a Strength Reaction Roll. On a failure, they take 4d10 Threat magic damage. On a success, they must mark a Stress.
What primary of their souls do you attack while this place tries to unmake them? What fundamental belief system or truth do you try to erase?

Outer Realms Predators - Action: Spend a Fear to summon an Outer Realms Abomination, an Outer Realms Corruptor, and 2d6 Outer Realms Thralls, who appear at Close range of a chosen PC. These entities are ravenous and have no interest in any of these adversaries, and you can spend an additional Fear to automatically succeed on that adversary's standard attack.
What half-consumed remnants of the shattered world do these monstrosities cast aside in pursuit of living flesh? What ragged survivors share their memories of loved ones lost in the gnashing jaws of unquestioning malice?

Disorienting Reality - Reaction: On a result with Fear, you can ask the PC to describe which of their fears the Chaos Realm evokes as a vision of reality unmakes and reconstitutes itself to the PC. Then ask one of their allies to describe a memory from home. What moment do they see? If it's a memory, how is it warped by this place? How hard will it be to hold on to the real memory?

entries.name: DIVINE USURPATION
entries.description:
Tier 4 Event
A massive ritual designed to breach the gates of the Hallows Above and unseat the New Gods themselves.
Impulses: Collect power, overawe, silence dissent
Difficulty: 20
Potential Adversaries: Arch-Necromancer, Fallen Shock Trooper, War Summoner, Oracle of Doom, Perfected Zombie

FEATURES
Final Preparations - Passive: The ritual must come to the spotlight. The spotlight one adversary as the Usurper seeking to overthrow the gods. Activate a Long-Term Countdown (3) as the Usurper assembles what they need to conduct the ritual. When it triggers, spotlight this environment to use the "Beginning of the End" move. While this environment remains in play, you can hold up to 15 Fear.
What does the Usurper still require: The heart of a High Seraph? The indentions of an ancient scepter? The loyalty of two elemental? The heartbroken tears of a pure soul?

Divine Awareness - Reaction: If the ritual is partially successful, they can spend 2 Hope to refresh an ability (normally limited by uses per rest, once per session).
What god favors you as you fight against this usurpation? How does your renewed power reflect their influence?

Deflect Abound - Action: Spend 2 Fear to summon 1d4+2 Fallen Shock Troops that appear within Close range of the Usurper to loyally serve them and the cause. You can also spend a Hope to use a "Group Attack" action.
Which High Fallen do these troops serve? Which god's flesh do they wish to feast upon?

Godslayer - Action: If the Divine Siege Countdown (see "Beginning of the End") has triggered, you can use this. spend a Fear as the Usurper playing one of the gods of the Hallows Above, feasting upon their power and growing stronger. The Usurper clears 2 HP, increases one Difficulty, damage, attack modifier, or give themselves a new feature from the slain god.
Which god meets their end? How does their "Final Preparations" countdown?

Usurper's new stolen power manifest?

Beginning of the End - Reaction: When the "Final Preparations" long-term countdown triggers, the Usurper begins hammering on the gates of the Hallows themselves. Activate a Divine Siege countdown (6). If it triggers, the Usurper performs a divine assault and lock down the countdown by 1. If the Usurper takes Major or greater damage, tick up the countdown by 1.
Which of the Old Gods first opens a pathway between the Mortal Realm and the Hallows Above to slay the gods and take their place. You gain a Fear for each unmarked HP the Usurper has. You can immediately use the "Godslayer" feature without spending Fear to make an additional GM move.
What divine being seeks to help resist this siege under a solicitor? What mortal virtues this blasphemy from afar?

Ritual Nexus - Reaction: On any failure with Fear against the Usurper, the PC must mark 1d4 Stress from the backlash of magical power.
What visions of fallows past torment you as your efforts fall short? How are these memories twisted by the Usurper?

entries.name: IMPERIAL COURT
entries.description:
Tier 4 Social
The intrigue domain of a powerful empire, lavishly appointed with silver treasures.
Impulses: Justify and perpetuate imperial rule, seduce rivals with treats, flaunt comfort
Difficulty: 20
Potential Adversaries: Bladed Guard, Courtesan, Knight of the Realm, Monarch, Spy

FEATURES
All Roads Lead Here - Passive: While in the Imperial Court, a PC has disadvantage on Presence Rolls made to take actions that don't fit the imperial way of life or support the empire's dominance.
Where do these proud, imperial decorations lead? What imposing alternative ways of living difficult? What obvious benefits for loyalty is the Fiction trying to offer in contrast to whatever the PCs seek?

Rival Vassals - Passive: The PCs can find imperial subjects, vassals, and sycophants at the court, each vying for favor. Each conversation is a delicate matter of politics and elevating their status above others. Some might be desperate to perform their trials, while others might want to open to discussions that verge on sedition.
How do they benefit from worshiping, and what has it cost them? What do they whisper when they think none can hear?

The Gravity of Empire - Action: Spend a Fear to present a PC with a golden opportunity or offer a path to a major goal in exchange for obeying or supporting the empire. The target must make a Presence Reaction Roll. On a failure, they must mark all their remaining armor slots with Stress. On a success, they gain Hope.
Stress, they must accept the offer or exile themselves from the empire. On a success, they must mark 1d8 Stress as they're taxed by temptation.
What do they PCs want so desperately they might consider throwing their principles away or their communities' fates in the interest of the greatest scheme?

Imperial Decree - Action: Spend a Fear to lock down a long-term countdown related to the empire's agenda by 1d4. If this triggers the countdown, a proclamation related to the agenda becomes law throughout the empire.
What display of power or transfer of wealth was needed to expedite this plan? What hope were dragged or gambled to make this happen?

Eyes Everywhere - Reaction: On a result with Fear, you can spend Hope to have spies in the court overhear the PC's conversation or talk within the court. A PC must succeed on an Instinct Reaction Roll to notice that this group has been overheard so they can try to intercept the witness before the PCs are exposed.
How have they already compromised this witness? Why is their first impulse to protect the empire, even if doesn't treat them well?

entries.name: NECROMANCER'S OSSUARY
entries.description:
Tier 4 Exploration
A dusty crypt with a library, twisting corridors, and abundant sarcophagi, splattered with the blood of defeated invaders.
Impulses: Confound intruders, drive out secrets best left buried, protect the necromancer from interruptions
Difficulty: 18
Potential Adversaries: Arch-Necromancer's Host (Perfected Zombie, Zombie Legion)

FEATURES
No Place for the Living - Passive: A feature or action that clears HP requires spending a Hope to use. If a already costs Hope, a PC must spend an additional Hope.
What does it feel like to try to heal in a place so antithetical to life?

Centurias of Knowledge - Passive: A PC can investigate the library and laboratory and make a Knowledge Reaction Roll. On success, they can ask a question about undeath, death, or ancient history.
What are the names of the tomes? What project is the necromancer working on and what does it communicate about their plans?

Skeletal Burst - Action: All targets within Close range of a point you choose in this environment must succeed on an Agility Reaction Roll. On a failure, they take 2d8+5 Physical damage as shrapnel as part of the ossuary detonates around them.
What ancient skeletal architecture is destroyed? What bone stick in your armor?

Aura of Death - Action: Once per scene, roll a d4. Each undead adversary within Close range of the Necromancer gains that HP and Stress equal to the result rolled. The undead can choose how that number is divided between HP and Stress.
How does their renewed vigor manifest? Do they look more lifelike or, paradoxically, are they more decayed but vigorous?

They Just Keep Coming - Action: Spend a Fear to summon 1d6 Rotted Zombies, two Perfected Zombies, or a Zombie Legion. They appear anywhere within Close range of a chosen PC.
Who are these people before they became the necromancer's pawns? What vestiges of those lives remain in the things they wear?

entries.name: STORY BEATS
entries.description: In storytelling, a beat is a moment that changes the trajectory of the narrative—a shift in plot, a significant action or reaction, an emotional revelation, or an important decision. Take turns with the players, narrating a beat and then letting them respond with their character's actions or dialogue. When preparing for a session, plan in terms of the moments that speak to each scene or sequence, rather than scripting specific details or exchanges.

entries.name: PREPARING COMBAT ENCOUNTERS
entries.description: Build the hurdles the PCs face around the question of "What helps tell the story?" Enemies, environments, and hazards are the tools for heightening tension and creating drama. Ensure that combat is being used to give players more information about the unfolding story, revealing the world, the plot, or the characters.

entries.name: BATTLES AND NARRATIVE
entries.description:
Dynamic battles create suspense by forcing players to choose between their values and their character's immediate motivations and weaknesses, and creating the crucible that the players use to forge their characters into legendary heroes. When preparing combat encounters:
• Consider the narrative themes of your scene or battleground to life
• Base adversaries' moves on their motives
• Use dynamic environments that shift the battleground to life
• Add enemies that can interact with the PCs' features and special abilities

entries.name: SESSION REWARDS
entries.description:
Reward players at the end of a session with:
• Useful information
• Story hooks
• Loot
• Gold
• Access to new equipment or enhancements

entries.name: CRAFTING SCENES
entries.description: Whenever you start a session, arrive at a new place, or change the situation, tell the players what they need to know by thinking with all of your senses and sharing something unique or unexpected about the fiction.

entries.name: ENGAGING YOUR PLAYERS
entries.description:
Keep your players engaged by:
• Rotating the Focus between the PCs
• Tying Together Story Elements
• Engaging Quest Players
• Using Visual Aids
• Encouraging Unguided Play
• Confronting the PCs with internal and external conflicts
• Raise the Stakes by Spending Fear
• Layering Goals Other than Attrition into Combat (see Table of Random Objectives on the next page)

entries.name: Table of Random Objectives
entries.description:
1d12	Objective
1	Acquire (obtain or steal) an important item or items.
2	Capture one or more of the opponents.
3	Activate a magical device.
4	Frame a character or tarnish their reputation.
5	Drive the opponent into a corner or ambush point.
6	Stop a magical ritual, legal ceremony, or time-sensitive spell.
7	Hold the line—keep the enemy from reaching a specific area or group.
8	Plant evidence or a tracking device on a target.
9	Secure a specific location ahead of another group's arrival.
10	Harass the opponent to deplete their resources or keep them occupied.
11	Destroy a piece of architecture, a statue, a shrine, or a weapon.
12	Investigate a situation to confirm or deny existing information.

entries.name: PHASED BATTLES
entries.description:
Make battles by shifting the nature of its enemies or environment mid-combat:
• Change the Terms of Engagement
• Alter the Environment
• Evolve the Opposition

entries.name: USING DOWNTIME
entries.description:
Use downtime scenes as a pressure release valve to vary the intensity of the story and give the PCs room to breathe.

Empower your players to frame their own downtime scenes. Ask the players what it looks like as they tend to their wounds or unwind together, encouraging them to take the reins and work with other players whose characters are involved.

entries.name: PROJECTS DURING DOWNTIME
entries.description:
The Work on a Project downtime move requires more GM input than other downtime moves and is best suited for long-term endeavors the PCs wish to undertake.

These projects are typically tracked using a Progress Countdown. When deciding the starting value of the countdown, consider the complexity of the project, the availability of relevant tools, and the impact of the project on the story.

Simple projects advance their countdown each time a player uses the Work on a Project move. But complex projects require a roll.

entries.name: EXTENDED DOWNTIME
entries.description: When you fast-forward the story across an extended period, use montages to illustrate the passage of time. You gain 1d6 Fear per PC and advance any long-term countdowns as appropriate.

entries.name: CAMPAIGN FRAMES
entries.description:
A campaign frame provides inspiration, tools, and mechanics to support a particular type of story at the table.

Each campaign frame has a complexity rating that indicates how much its mechanics deviate from or expand upon the Daggerheart core rules.

Each campaign frame includes the following sections.
• A pitch to present to players
• Suggestions and guidance on tone, feel, themes, and touchstones
• An overview of the campaign's background
• Guidance for fitting communities, ancestries, and classes into the setting
• Principles for players and GMs to focus on during the campaign
• Unique setting distinctions
• An inciting incident to launch the campaign
• Special mechanics to use during the campaign
• Questions to consider during session zero

You can find each campaign frame map in the appendix of the core rulebook or at www.daggerheart.com/downloads.

entries.name: THE WITHERWILD
entries.description:
When an invading nation attacks an ancient forest deity, a virulent overgrowth spreads throughout the land.

Designed by Carlos Cisco, Rowan Hall, & Spenser Starke

COMPLEXITY RATING: ●

THE PITCH
Read this section to your players to introduce them to the campaign.

Farewick was once a place of great abundance and peace—dangerous to those unfamiliar with its land, but a cornucopia to those who respected its ways. When Haven invaded the wilds and forced the lord into eternal spring, a dangerous bloom known as the Witherwild took hold and now threatens the lives of all who live there. In a Witherwild campaign, you'll play unlikely heroes from humble beginnings who are reckoning with their newfound duty to save Farewick's people from dangerous corruption.

TONE & FEEL
Adventurous, Dynamic, Epic, Heroic, Thrilling, Uncanny, Whimsical

THEMES
Cultural Clash, Ends Justify Means, Grief, People vs. Nature, Transformation and Change, Survival

TOUCHSTONES
Princess Mononoke, The Legend of Zelda, The Dark Crystal, Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind

OVERVIEW
If your group decides to play this campaign, give your players the following information before character creation.

Farewick is a wild and untamed land, long avoided by outside forces. The woods are dark and twisting, filled with Faint Divinities who perform small miracles and services for its people. Wondrous creatures roam ancient, overgrown paths to their ruin. Seemingly harmless expanses of field hide bogs that engulf entire armies, consuming heavy artillery as quickly as it can cross the border. This wilderness fosters hardy people who are bold enough to raise families where others have not and strictly follow the unique rules that keep them safe. These Wickings remained insulated from their neighbors' wars until plague forced the people of Haven to desperate acts.

Haven was once the most powerful force in the region; for centuries, the high stone walls into the capital bore the phrase "The Godless Gate." While the original meaning has been

entries.name: ADDITIONAL GM GUIDANCE
entries.description: This section provides additional guidance for preparing and running a session of Daggerheart.

entries.name: Random Objectives Table
entries.description:
1d12	Objective
1	Acquire (obtain or steal) an important item or items.
2	Capture one or more of the opponents.
3	Activate a magical device.
4	Frame a character or tarnish their reputation.
5	Drive the opponent into a corner or ambush point.
6	Stop a magical ritual, legal ceremony, or time-sensitive spell.
7	Hold the line—keep the enemy from reaching a specific area or group.
8	Plant evidence or a tracking device on a target.
9	Secure a specific location ahead of another group's arrival.
10	Harass the opponent to deplete their resources or keep them occupied.
11	Destroy a piece of architecture, a statue, a shrine, or a weapon.
12	Investigate a situation to confirm or deny existing information.

entries.name: THE PITCH
entries.description:
Read this section to your players to introduce them to the campaign.

Fanewick was once a place of great abundance and peace—dangerous to those unfamiliar with the land, but a cornucopia to those who respected its ways. When Haven invaded the wilds and forced the god of the eternal spring, a dangerous bloom known as the Witherwild took hold and now threatens the lives of all who live there. In a Witherwild campaign, you'll play unlikely heroes from humble beginnings who are reckoning with their newfound duty to save Fanewick's people from dangerous corruption.

entries.name: TONE & FEEL
entries.description: Adventurous, Dynamic, Epic, Heroic, Thrilling, Uncanny, Whimsical

entries.name: THEMES
entries.description: Cultural Clash, Ends Justify Means, Grief, People vs. Nature, Transformation and Change, Survival

entries.name: TOUCHSTONES
entries.description: Princess Mononoke, The Legend of Zelda, The Dark Crystal, Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind

entries.name: RIDEBORNE AND HIGHBORNE
entries.description:
In Fanewick, knowledge is the most valuable commodity, and those who have expertise have the most power. This economy of information functions primarily through gifting and trading, and wisdom would be considered acceptable collateral for loans. The most isolated but specialized community members are the wealthiest in Fanewick and might be smaller, distinct groups of hunters, historians, or artisans within larger villages.

What knowledge did your community teach you that you must now protect or share?

What are you able to accomplish because of your upbringing that others outside your community do not understand?

You once traded important knowledge for something terrible. What did you impart and what did you gain in return?

In Haven, the wealthiest members are highborne, having inherited riches built in the wallets of their forebears. While the highborne of Haven were more insulated from the Serpent's Sickness, none were immune.

Who have you lost to the Serpent's Sickness? How did it affect you?

How did your family make their wealth? Have you rejected or embraced their core set of ethics?

You grew up in a world of abundance but were kept from learning something about the world. What was this knowledge and when did you discover it?

entries.name: RIDEBORNE, UNDERBORNE, AND WILDBORNE
entries.description:
Characters from these communities were likely raised in Fanewick's deadly environs, which have only become more dangerous as the Witherwild grows. Each community has unique customs, some freely shared and others kept secret, that allow them safe passage through dangerous terrain and the Faint Divinities' territories.

In exchange for protection from the Witherwild for themselves and their families, some Wicklings from these groups have been forced to take jobs as farmers in Haven's towns. Though it is backbreaking work performed for a nation destroying their land, the farmers are offered safety by the Haven fires that burn back the ever-creeping Witherwild.

entries.name: What Faint Divinities do you give tribute to, and how have you been rewarded in kind?
entries.description:
You displaced a god of Fanewick. What did you do, and what misfortune befell you or a loved one?

You fled the place of your birth. What circumstances drove you to leave?

You recently traveled by sea over a vast distance. What went terribly wrong on that passage, and how has it changed you?

Growing up on the sea taught you a skill few have. What did you learn, and how did it save your life?

You used to sail with a companion. Who were they, and how do you remain connected?

entries.name: CLANKS
entries.description: Clanks from Haven are commonly made from iron and steel, while clanks from Fanewick are typically constructed from wood and stone.

entries.name: DRAKONA, FAUNS, FIRBOLGS, AND INFERNIS
entries.description: After the Witherwild corrupted Fanewick, some drakona, fauns, firbolgs, and infernis have noticed their horns growing faster and longer.

entries.name: GALAPA AND RIBBETS
entries.description: Many families of galapa and ribbets who lived in the bogs of Fanewick were displaced when Haven's army invaded.

entries.name: Havenites and the Serpent's Sickness
entries.description: Anyone, but especially those from Haven, may carry the Serpent's Sickness, which stiffens their movements and gives them limited time to survive without a cure.

entries.name: CLASSES
entries.description: All classes are available, but some have unique aspects within a Witherwild campaign. As needed, provide the following information to your players.

entries.name: Druids, Rangers, and Sorcerers
entries.description: Druids, rangers, and sorcerers are commonly found throughout Fanewick. If players choose one of these classes, they should consider how their character's connection to the natural world might be impacted by the Witherwild.

entries.name: WARRIORS AND WIZARDS
entries.description: Warriors and wizards are prevalent in Haven. A large wizarding school is located in Arcus, and haven's military, the Haven Army is largely composed of warriors and School of War wizards. If a PC is either of these classes, consider their personal relationship to the Haven Army.

entries.name: VENGEANCE GUARDIAN
entries.description: Many Wicklings who seek revenge on Haven or to expel them from Fanewick become Vengeance guardians. If players choose this subclass, they should consider what ideals or institutions their character protects.

entries.name: SYNDICATE ROGUE
entries.description: The Haven Army stands apart from Fanewick communities to gain information on planned attacks and manipulate public perception. When selecting the Syndicate rogue subclass, players should consider how their character might be connected to or impacted by these covert operations.

entries.name: PLAYER PRINCIPLES
entries.description: If your group decides to play this campaign, give your players the following information before character creation.

entries.name: MAKE THE INVASION PERSONAL
entries.description: Consider how Haven's invasion of Fanewick deeply impacts your character. Let these details inform what they want and what they're willing to do to get it. Everyone has lost a loved one to the Witherwild or the Serpent's Sickness, and more than likely, the invasion or the spreading corruption has caused your character to act in ways they aren't proud of, or transformed personal truths they once believed were unshakable.

entries.name: TREAT DEATH WITH IMPORTANCE
entries.description: Dying a life should not be done without consideration, desperation, and consequences. As your character journeys, they should remember those they've lost and those they've slain, allowing each death to affect their choices in the future.

entries.name: EMBRACE VULNERABILITY
entries.description: Find your character's humanity and showcase it whenever you can. They should be as vulnerable during quiet moments as they are vicious during violent ones, opening up to their allies even if they close themself off to everyone else.

entries.name: PAINT THE WORLD IN CONTRAST
entries.description:
Subvert expectations by giving space for the nightmarish to charm and the beautiful to terrify. Give the PCs visions of the natural beauty of Fanewick that has run roughshod across the region, breaking the bounds of the bogs and forests and devastating homes and communities. Show the grin and ordered life behind Haven's walls, but also show the culture, customs, and lives of all the people contained within them.

Contrast the capricious nature of the Faint Divinities with the devotion of the Wicklings and Havenites fighting to save their home. In a place of long nights and days, dual seasons, and two nations in conflict, look for ways to highlight bold dualities.

entries.name: SHOW THEM TRUE DANGER
entries.description: Though it may be tragic, boldly harm is not the worst tragedy that can befall the characters. To illustrate what's truly at stake, use the PCs' origins in Fanewick or Haven to show them what they stand to lose, be it their homes, family, friends, communities, or even their core truths and beliefs. The events of your campaign should challenge the most meaningful aspects of their past—when your players create and are invested in their character's backstory, they have something to fight for.

entries.name: OFFER ALTERNATIVES TO VIOLENCE
entries.description: By illuminating nonviolent approaches to problem-solving, the PCs can help break the brutal cycle that binds this world. For example, showing compassion—rather than swooping—to a corrupted beast can free them from the Witherwild. Similarly, the conflict between Haven and Fanewick shouldn't only be solved with blood and blade. What threatens both could also unify them. Give the PCs opportunities to mend wounds, right past wrongs, and heal the rift between Haven and Fanewick from within.

entries.name: CREATE MULTIDIMENSIONAL ALLIES AND ADVERSARIES
entries.description:
No adversary or ally is all good or all bad, and they each have multidimensional lives. Even the most congenial ally should be hiding darkness inside them, hoping their failings will go undiscovered. Those painted as evil should sometimes do the right thing, even if it's for the wrong reason. Just because a PC or NPC is from Haven doesn't make them a villain, and just because they're from Fanewick doesn't make them good.

Finding the nuance, complications, and deeper motivations of the people of Haven and Fanewick will lead to richer interactions, more complex moral choices, and a deeper sense of attachment to the world for the players.

entries.name: DISTINCTIONS
entries.description: Use this information to prepare your campaign. You can also share it with your players as needed.

entries.name: THE WEEKS OF DAY AND NIGHT
entries.description:
The day and night cycle occurs weekly, meaning the sun rises for an entire week before setting into a nighttime of the same length. This has an impact on everything from agriculture to trade, as many merchants refuse to travel through the night.

But this cycle doesn't just affect Wicklings. It's dictated how Fanewick's wondrous ecology has evolved. Days and nights are accompanied by entirely different natural soundscapes as Fanewick's nocturnal denizens sleep through the long days while their botanical neighbors awaken and their diurnal counterparts sleep. The plants similarly live on this strange day-night loop, and many live out their entire life cycles in a week. Sun roses vines make a thorny ascent to the top of the tree canopy over the course of the week, only to release in the light of the moon. Conversely, night bloom, a bioluminescent flower that tempts the rest of Fanewick with its sweet scent, only blossoms when evening falls, illuminating patches of forest—a godsend to anyone forced to travel in the dark.

entries.name: THE SERPENT'S SICKNESS
entries.description:
The plague sweeping across Haven spares no one. Since the initial outbreak, Haven officials have worked tirelessly to find a cure for the Serpent's Sickness, but their research effort has done little to stop its spread. Due to the rarity of crimson lady's veil, many citizens began to carry or consume all manner of flowers and snake sheddings in an attempt to protect or cure themselves.

In the initial stages, victims are afflicted with a hacking cough laden with dust that resembles powdered bone. Their is scaled rash erupts across their skin, cracking their flesh in a unique and painful pattern that resembles a snake's skin. Not long after, the victims' bodies harden until their organs solidify and cease to function. The final stages of the Serpent's Sickness is so quick to transform into statues where they stand, leaving all of Haven a cenotaph to a once-great power.

entries.name: LADY'S VEIL
entries.description:
These small flowers grow in patches of sun throughout Fanewick. For every ten thousand blossoms of white-petaled Lady's veil there might be one with a crimson center. They have long been used in herbal teas across the region, with the crimson flowers holding a place in specific ceremonies. Few were left, so they were only foraged until the Havenites discovered crimson lady's veil—the only known cure for Serpent's Sickness.

Thus, the Haven Army invaded Fanewick and began cultivating the flowers on massive farms that have overtaken the region's bogs. Though the theft of the Reaping Eye has created an indefinite growing season, the red flowers remain just as rare, and Fanewick is permeated by a sickly sweet smell as the Haven Army races to mass-produce a cure for the Serpent's Sickness.

entries.name: THE GODS OF FANEWICK
entries.description:
Gods in this land aren't ethereal concepts that exist in another realm; instead, they wander the land as incarnate beings, residing in both the natural world as well as within homes and hearths. They are physical beings who live among mortal lives. Many communities, and some larger families, even have their own small god or tutelary spirit around which their lives revolve.

Belief and worship take on a different meaning in Fanewick—they are a constant push and pull between communities and their specific neighbors. The gods must curry worship from mortals, often by performing small miracles, acts of service, or by testing their resolve with tricks and curses. Mortals rely on the small blessings from the gods to keep them safe when traveling and working in Fanewick. The most powerful and widely acknowledged Faint Divinity is Nikta, Master of Seasons, Nikta, as the loss of her eye had the most recent and visible impact on everyone in Fanewick.

Fanewick's various divinities each have their own opinion concerning the Witherwild corruption and the invasion of nature's newfound dominance, many of the lesser deities are wary of how out of balance Fanewick has become. While their behavior and motives may be inscrutable, these deities are more accessible than gods who watch from afar.

Følg, Stacker of Stones: A small god who favors tribute in the form of smooth, stacked river rocks. Under his power, stones can be shaped into tools and weapons, although they can be easily harvested. He is largely uninformed about the problems Fanewick faces, and he prefers it that way.

Hyacynth, They Who Spread Small Blossoms: A friend of bees and tiny flowers who has embraced the Witherwild after seeing their influence and domain swell (literally) to immense proportions. They're enjoying this rampant growth and do not see a reason to end it.

entries.name: Ikla, the Sky Painter
entries.description: Ikla's artistic prowess and talent are unmatched, painting the skies of each weekly sunrise and sunset over Fanewick in a myriad of scintillating colors. They could not be lazier the rest of the week, and their demeanor is exceedingly unpleasant, offended that anyone would ask a favor of them during their time off.

entries.name: Oove, the Watcher of the Night Bloom
entries.description: He is one of the few local gods who has never been seen, but he is often heard in the wilds at night, whispering directions to lost travelers, pointing them to the nearest source of night bloom. Locals claim he leads those who displease him or harm nocturnal creatures into dangerous terrain. It's rumored that he watches over Fanewick nights from his home in the moon.

entries.name: Qui'Gar, Whispered Sweetness for a Thorny End
entries.description: A quiet god who speaks over the deaths of those who pass in Fanewick's graveyards. Despite the fact that the town doesn't receive many visitors, but the Witherwild's corruptive growth has made her job harder and expanded her quiet existence.

entries.name: Rokkin, the Wandering Horn
entries.description: Rokkin watches over the many herds of horned creatures that reside in Fanewick. In happier times, he preserved the balance of nature and civilization, preventing overhunting. But since the corruption of the Witherwild has taken hold, he's seen his herds change into unrecognizable, dangerous beasts.

entries.name: CORRUPTION FROM THE WITHERWILD
entries.description:
Adversaries and environments in this campaign can gain an additional type called Withered. When an adversary is Withered, describe how the Witherwild changed or impacted how they would normally appear or operate. You also need about 20 Wither tokens (these can be the same as the tokens you use for Fear).

Any time a PC takes Severe damage from a Withered adversary or environment, gain a Wither token and place it on that PC's character sheet. Wither you do, the PC must roll with Fear. At the start of a PC's turn, if they have any Wither tokens on their sheet, they immediately gain a scar and clear all tokens, describing how the Witherwild changes them permanently.

At the end of each session, clear all Wither tokens from the PCs' sheets and gain an equal amount of Fear. If a character ever dies with Wither tokens on their sheet, their body is permanently taken over by the Witherwild.

entries.name: SESSION ZERO QUESTIONS
entries.description:
Ask any of these questions to your players, or make your own.

What dangerous animal comes out during the week of night that isn't out during the week of day?

What unique trait (such as appearance, smell, or taste) does anything tainted by the Witherwild have?

What superstitions does your character or their community have about traversing Fanewick during the long nights?

Your character has witnessed something beautiful that came from the Witherwild. What is it and how has it transformed their view of the corruption?

entries.name: RUNE WARD
entries.description:
Level 1 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 0

You trace a deeply personal trinket that can be infused with arcane magic for use in later spells (describe what it is). Describe what it is and why it's important to you. The ward you inscribe can spend a Hope to reduce incoming damage by 1d8.

If the Ward Die result is 5, the ward's power ends after it reduces damage this turn. It can be recharged for free on your next rest.

entries.name: UNLEASH CHAOS
entries.description:
Level 1 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 1

At the beginning of a session, place a number of tokens equal to your Spellcast trait on the card.

Make a Spellcast Roll against a target within Far range and spend any number of tokens to channel raw energy from within yourself and the world around you. Deal a number of d10s equal to the tokens you spent and deal that much magic damage to the target. Mark a Stress to replenish this card with tokens (up to your Spellcast trait).

At the end of each session, clear all unspent tokens.

entries.name: WALL WALK
entries.description:
Level 1 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 1

Spend a Hope to allow a creature you can touch to climb on walls and ceilings as easily as walking on the ground. This lasts until the end of the scene or you cast Wall Walk again.

entries.name: CINDER GRASP
entries.description:
Level 2 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 1

Make a Spellcast Roll against a target within Melee range. On a success, your hands instantly burst into flames, taking 1d20+3 magic damage, and is temporarily lit On Fire.

When a creature acts while On Fire, they must take an extra 2d6 magic damage if they are still On Fire at the end of their action.

entries.name: FLOATING EYE
entries.description:
Level 2 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 0

Spend 1 Hope to create a single, small floating orb that you can move anywhere within Very Far range. While this spell is active, you can see through the orb as though you're looking through it, using your own senses. You can see using your own senses and seeing through the orb freely. If the orb takes damage or moves out of range, the spell ends.

entries.name: COUNTERSPELL
entries.description:
Level 3 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 2

You can interrupt a magical effect taking place by making a Spellcast Roll using your Spellcast trait. On a success, the effect stops and any consequences are avoided, and this card is placed in your vault.

entries.name: FLIGHT
entries.description:
Level 3 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 1

Make a Spellcast Roll (15). On a success, place a number of tokens equal to your Agility on this card (minimum 1). When you make an
action roll while flying, spend a token from this card. After the action that spends the last token is resolved,
your character falls to the ground directly below you.

entries.name: BLINK OUT
entries.description:
Level 4 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 1

Make a Spellcast Roll (12). On a success, spend a Hope to teleport to another point you can see within Far range. If any willing creatures are within Very Close range, spend an additional Hope for each creature to bring them with you.

entries.name: PRESERVATION BLAST
entries.description:
Level 4 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 2

Make a Spellcast Roll against all targets within Melee range. Targets you succeed against are frozen back in Far range and take 6d13 magic damage using your Spellcast trait.

entries.name: CHAIN LIGHTNING
entries.description:
Level 5 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 1

Mark 2 Stress to make a Spellcast Roll, unleashing lightning on all targets within Close range. Targets you succeed against take 4d20+3 damage. Additionally, make another Spellcast Roll. Targets who fail take 2d8+4 magic damage. Additional adversaries not already targeted by Chain Lightning and within Close range of previous targets who took damage must also make the reaction roll. Targets who fail take 2d8+4 magic damage. This can continue until there are no more adversaries within range.

entries.name: PREMONITION
entries.description:
Level 8 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 2
You can channel arcane energy to have visions of the future. Once per long rest, immediately after the GM conveys the consequences of a roll you made, you can rescind the move and consequences like they never happened and make another move instead.

entries.name: RIFT WALKER
entries.description:
Level 6 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 2
Make a Spellcast Roll (15). On a success, you place an arcane marking on any location you can see within Very Far range. Any time you successfully cast Rift Walker, a rift in space opens up, providing safe passage back to the exact spot where the marking was placed. This rift stays open until you choose to close it or you cast another spell.
You can drop the spell at any time to cast Rift Walker again and place the marking somewhere new.

entries.name: TELEKINESIS
entries.description:
Level 5 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 0
Make a Spellcast Roll against a target within Far range. On a success, you use your mind to move them anywhere within Far range of their original position. You can throw the lifted target as an attack by making an additional Spellcast Roll against the second target you're trying to attack. On a success, deal d12+4 physical damage to the second target using your Proficiency. This spell then ends.

entries.name: ARCANA TOUCHED
entries.description:
Level 7 Arcana Ability
Recall Cost: 1
When 4 or more of the domain cards in your loadout are from the Arcana domain, gain the following benefits:
• +2 bonus to your Spellcast Rolls
• Once per rest, you can switch the results of your Hope and Fear Dice.

entries.name: CLOAKING BLAST
entries.description:
Level 7 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 2
After making a successful Spellcast Roll to cast a different spell, you can spend a Hope to become Cloaked. While Cloaked, you remain unseen if you are stationary when an adversary moves to where they would normally see you. When you move into or within an adversary's line of sight or make an attack, you are no longer Cloaked.

entries.name: ARCANE REFLECTION
entries.description:
Level 8 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 1
Once per long rest, when you fail a Spellcast Roll, spend any number of Hope to roll that many dice. If any roll a 6, the attack is reflected back to the caster, dealing the damage to them instead.

entries.name: CONFUSING AURA
entries.description:
Level 8 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 2
Make a Spellcast Roll (14). Once per long rest on a success, you create a layer of illusion over your body that makes it hard to tell exactly where you are. Mark any number of Stress to make that many additional layers. When an adversary makes an attack against you, roll a number of dice equal to the layers you have. If any roll a 6, the attack fails and that one layer of the aura is destroyed and the attack fails. If all the results are 4 or lower, you take the damage and this spell ends.

entries.name: EARTHQUAKE
entries.description:
Level 9 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 2
Make a Spellcast Roll (16). Once per rest on a success, all targets within Very Far range who aren't flying must make a Reaction Roll (15). Targets who fail take 3d10+8 physical damage and become temporarily Vulnerable. Targets who succeed take half damage.
Additionally, when you succeed on the Spellcast Roll, all terrain within Very Far range becomes difficult to move through and structures within this range might sustain damage or crumble.

entries.name: SENSORY PROJECTION
entries.description:
Level 9 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 0
Once per rest, make a Spellcast Roll (13). On a success, drop into a vision that lets you clearly see and hear any place you have been before as though you are standing there in person. You cannot interact with anything, but you are not constrained by the physics or impediments of a physical body. This spell cannot be detected by mundane or magical means. You drop out of this vision upon taking damage or casting another spell.

entries.name: ADJUST REALITY
entries.description:
Level 10 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 1
After you or a willing ally make any roll, you can spend 5 Hope to alter the numerical result of that roll to a result of your choice instead. The result must be plausible within the range of the dice.

entries.name: FALLING SKY
entries.description:
Level 10 Arcana Spell
Recall Cost: 1
Make a Spellcast Roll against all adversaries within Far range. Mark any number of Stress to make shards of arcana rain down from above. Targets you succeed against take d20+2 magic damage for each Stress marked.

entries.name: GET BACK UP
entries.description:
Level 1 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 1
When you take Severe damage, you can mark a Stress to reduce the severity by one threshold.

entries.name: NOT GOOD ENOUGH
entries.description:
Level 1 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 1
When you roll your damage dice, you can reroll any 1s or 2s.

entries.name: WHIRLWIND
entries.description:
Level 1 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 0
When you make a successful attack against a target within Melee range, you can spend a Hope to use the attack against all other targets within Very Close range. All additional adversaries you succeed against with this ability take half damage.

entries.name: A SOLDIER'S BOND
entries.description:
Level 2 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 1
Once per long rest, when you compliment someone or ask them about something they're good at, you can both gain 3 Hope.

entries.name: RECKLESS
entries.description:
Level 2 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 1
Mark a Stress to gain advantage on an attack.

entries.name: SCRAMBLE
entries.description:
Level 2 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 1
Once per rest, when a creature within Melee range would deal damage to you, you can make the attack and safely move out of Melee range of the enemy.

entries.name: EXPERIENCED FIGHTER
entries.description:
Level 3 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 1
You can use a different character trait for an equipped weapon, rather than the trait the weapon calls for.
When you deal damage, you can mark a Stress to use the maximum result of one of your damage dice instead of rolling it.

entries.name: DEADLY FOCUS
entries.description:
Level 4 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 2
Once per rest, you can apply all your focus toward a target of your choice. Until you attack another creature, you defeat the target, or the battle ends, gain a +1 bonus to your Proficiency.

entries.name: FORTIFIED ARMOR
entries.description:
Level 4 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 0
While you are wearing armor, gain a +2 bonus to your damage thresholds.

entries.name: CHAMPION'S EDGE
entries.description:
Level 5 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 1
When you critically succeed on an attack, you can spend up to 3 Hope and choose one of the following options for each Hope spent:
• You clear a Hit Point
• You clear an Armor Slot
• The target must mark an additional Hit Point
You can't choose the same option more than once.

entries.name: VITALITY
entries.description:
Level 5 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 0
When you choose this card, permanently gain two of the following benefits:
• One Stress slot
• One Hit Point slot
• +2 bonus to your damage thresholds
Then place this card in your vault permanently.

entries.name: BATTLE-HARDENED
entries.description:
Level 6 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 2
Once per long rest when you would make a Death Move, you can spend 6 Hope to clear a Hit Point instead.

entries.name: RAGE UP
entries.description:
Level 6 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 1
Before you make an attack, you can mark a Stress to gain a bonus to your damage roll equal to twice your Strength.
You can use this again per attack.

entries.name: BLADE TOUCHED
entries.description:
Level 7 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 1
When 4 or more of the domain cards in your loadout are from the Blade domain, gain the following benefits:
• +2 bonus to your attack rolls
• +4 bonus to your Severe damage threshold

entries.name: GLANCING BLOW
entries.description:
Level 7 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 1
When you fail an attack, you can mark a Stress to deal weapon damage using half your Proficiency.

entries.name: BATTLE CRY
entries.description:
Level 8 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 2
Once per long rest, while you're charging into danger, you can muster a rousing call that inspires your allies. All allies who can hear you can mark a Stress and gain a Hope. Additionally, your allies gain advantage on attack rolls until you or an ally rolls a failure with Fear.

entries.name: FRENZY
entries.description:
Level 1 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 3
Once per long rest, you can go into a Frenzy until there are no more adversaries within sight.
While Frenzied, you can't use Armor Slots, and you gain a +10 bonus to your damage rolls and a +8 bonus to your Severe damage threshold.

entries.name: GORE AND GLORY
entries.description:
Level 1 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 2
When you critically succeed on a weapon attack, gain an additional Hope or clear an additional Stress.
Additionally, when you deal enough damage to defeat an enemy, gain a Hope or clear a Stress.

entries.name: REAPER'S STRIKE
entries.description:
Level 3 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 3
Once per long rest, spend a Hope to make an attack roll. The GM tells you which targets within range it would succeed against. Choose one of these targets and force them to mark 5 Hit Points.

entries.name: BATTLE MONSTER
entries.description:
Level 10 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 0
When you make a successful attack against an adversary, you can mark 4 Stress to force the target to mark a number of Hit Points equal to the total. Additionally, you currently have marked instead of rolling for damage.

entries.name: ONSLAUGHT
entries.description:
Level 10 Blade Ability
Recall Cost: 3
When you successfully make an attack with your weapon, you never deal damage beneath a target's Major damage threshold (the target always marks a minimum of 2 Hit Points).
Additionally, when a creature within your weapon's range deals damage to an ally with an attack that doesn't include you, you can mark a Stress to force them to make a Reaction Roll (15). On a failure, the target must mark a Hit Point.

entries.name: BOOST
entries.description:
Level 4 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 1
Mark 4 Stress to boost off a willing ally within Close range, fling yourself into the air, and perform an aerial attack against a target within Far range. You have advantage on the attack, add a d10 to the damage roll, and end your move within Melee range of the target.

entries.name: REDIRECT
entries.description:
Level 4 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 1
When an attack is made against you, from beyond Melee range and you can see the attacker, add to your Proficiency. If any roll 16, you can mark a Stress to redirect the attack to damage an adversary within Very Close range instead.

entries.name: KNOW THY ENEMY
entries.description:
Level 5 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 1
When observing a creature, you can make an Instinct Roll against them. On a success, spend a Hope and ask the GM for one set of information about the target from the following options:
• Their unmarked Hit Points and Stress.
• Their Difficulty and damage thresholds.
• Their tactics and standard attack damage dice.
• Their features and Experiences.
Additionally on a success, you can mark a Stress to remove a Fear from the GM's Fear Pool.

entries.name: SIGNATURE MOVE
entries.description:
Level 5 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 1
Name and describe your signature combat move. Once per long rest, when you perform this signature move as part of an action you're taking, you can roll a d20 as your Hope Die. On a success, clear a Stress.

entries.name: RAPID RIPOSTE
entries.description:
Level 6 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 0
When an attack is made against you from within Melee range, you can mark 2 Stress and seize the opportunity to deal the weapon damage of one of your active weapons to the attacker.

entries.name: RECOVERY
entries.description:
Level 6 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 1
During a short rest, you can choose a long rest downtime move instead. You can spend a Hope to let an ally do the same.

entries.name: BONE-TOUCHED
entries.description:
Level 7 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 2
When 4 or more of the domain cards in your loadout are from the Bone domain, gain the following benefits:
• +1 bonus to Agility
• Once per rest, you can spend 3 Hope to cause an attack that succeeded against you to fail instead.

entries.name: CRUEL PRECISION
entries.description:
Level 8 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 1
When you make a successful attack with a weapon, gain a bonus to your damage roll equal to either your Finesse or Agility.

entries.name: BREAKING BLOW
entries.description:
Level 8 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 3
When you make a successful attack, you can mark a Stress to make the next successful attack against that same target deal an extra 2d12 damage.

entries.name: WRANGLE
entries.description:
Level 8 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 1
Make an Agility Roll against all targets within Close range. Spend a Hope to move targets you succeed against, and any willing allies within Close range, to another point within Close range.

entries.name: ON THE BRINK
entries.description:
Level 9 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 1
When you have 2 or fewer Hit Points unmarked, you don't take Minor damage.

entries.name: SPLINTERING STRIKE
entries.description:
Level 9 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 3
Spend a Hope and make an attack against all adversaries within your weapon's range. Once per long rest, roll an attack against any targets, roll your weapon's damage and distribute that damage however you wish between the targets you succeeded against. Before you deal damage to each target, roll an additional damage die and add the result to the damage you deal to them.

entries.name: DEATHRUN
entries.description:
Level 10 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 1
Spend 3 Hope to run a straight path through the battlefield to a point within Far range, making an attack against all adversaries within your weapon's range along the path. Choose the order in which you deal damage to the targets you succeeded against. For the first, roll your weapon damage with advantage. For each subsequent target, reduce the weapon damage roll and deal the remaining damage to the next target. Continue to remove a die for each subsequent target until you have no more damage dice or adversaries.
You can't target the same adversary more than once per attack.

entries.name: SWIFT STEP
entries.description:
Level 10 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 2
When an attack made against you fails, clear a Stress. If you can't clear a Stress, gain a Hope.

entries.name: DEFT MANEUVERS
entries.description:
Level 1 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 0
Once per rest, mark a Stress to sprint anywhere within Far range without making an Agility Roll to get there.
If you end this movement within Melee range of an adversary who can see you, you can make an attack against them; gain a +1 bonus to the attack roll.

entries.name: LAST FOAMING
entries.description:
Level 1 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 1
When you're targeted by an attack made from beyond Melee range, you can mark a Stress to roll a d4 and gain a bonus to your Evasion equal to the result against the attack.

entries.name: UNTOUCHABLE
entries.description:
Level 1 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 1
Gain a bonus to your Evasion equal to half your Agility.

entries.name: FEROCITY
entries.description:
Level 2 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 2
When you cause an adversary to mark 1 or more Hit Points, you can spend 2 Hope to increase your Evasion by the number of Hit Points they marked. This bonus lasts until after the next attack made against you.

entries.name: STRATEGIC APPROACH
entries.description:
Level 2 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 1
After a long rest, place a number of tokens equal to your Instinct into your Bone pool somewhere. Then, draw a number of cards equal to the number of allies within Close range of an adversary and make an attack against them; you can spend one token to choose one of the following options:
• You make the attack with advantage.
• You clear a Stress on an ally within Melee range of the adversary.
• You add a d8 to your damage roll.
When you take a long rest, clear all unspent tokens.

entries.name: BRACE
entries.description:
Level 3 Bone Ability
Recall Cost: 1
When you mark an Armor Slot to reduce incoming damage, you can mark a Stress to mark an additional Armor Slot.
